ranked.transcript: Today we're checking out the Bose QC Ultra Gen 2. Now Gen 1 still has some of the strongest noise cancelling, but were plagued with issues. Poor connectivity, buzzing in one earbud, and some of the worst call quality. Spoiler, that's basically all been fixed on Gen 2. But as usual, today I'll be scoring and ranking the buds in a total of 10 categories. Now, the retail price is $299. They cost me $380 on sale in Australia. But let's get into category 1, comfort. I'm scoring the QC2 an 8.5 out of 10. It's basically the same fit as Gen One. You get three sizes of ear tips, three sizes of wings, but the ear tips now have a cover on them, so less chance of wax build up on the drivers, but it's the same moderately deep fit. The ovalshaped ear tips are quite comfortable. They should work fine whether you have large ears or small ears. Now, category two is a security of the fit. I'm scoring them an 8.5 here as well since they're one of the few buds that actually have a wing tip, which just gives you that extra peace of mind when exercising. Even when I don't use the wings, the buds still lock in place great whether I'm weight training or running. But it's still nice to have a wing so you won't have to adjust your earbuds as often. I've only got two buds scored higher on my spreadsheet as they do have a more stable wing, but they are exercise focused earbuds. Still, on the QC2, you get an IPX4 water resistant rating. All right, category 3 is case and battery life. Score on the QC2 a 7.5 out of 10. Again, same as Gen 1, 6 hours of bud battery, 24 hours in total with the case, and that's with noise cancelling on. Bose does advertise 4 hours of battery if you use spatial audio. You also get the same fast charge. So, chuck the buds in the case for 20 minutes. You're going to get two hours of playback on the earbuds. What's new on Gen 2, though, is that the case can now be charged wirelessly. Really should have been there back before the Ultra even came out. But anyway, it's here now. The case itself is also identical to Gen 1. It's not the most compact, but thin enough so you can still chuck it in your pocket. Here it is compared to one of the thinnest cases, the AirPods Pro 3. All right, category four is controls. score on the QC2 an 8.5 out of 10. Again, exactly the same as Gen One. I originally had them scored an eight since you don't get the best customization, but the main thing here is that you get swipe control for volume, which I do prioritize with my scoring. Now, I still prefer squeeze and swipe controls. So, if your fingers are sweaty or clammy, it can kind of grip on the touch surface, but it works well enough. So, the great thing about swipe controls, even with one earbud, you can control everything. Play, pause, track control, volume, and noise cancelling. You do get some light customization of the long hold. You can add spatial audio, Bluetooth device switch, voice assistant, and quick access to Spotify. The wearing detection here is also the same. You don't get skin detection, and there's still no option to mute your mic on a phone call. All right, category 5 is connectivity. Again, scoring the QC2 an 8.5 out of 10. Again, same as gen one, you got Bluetooth 5.3, SBC, AAC, AEX adaptive, and lossless. You also have Google Fast Pair, and Microsoft Swift pair. You can also use either earbud with the other in the case and it's going to work in mono mode so you hear left and right audio channel out of that one bud. You also get multi-point connection so you can have two devices connected to the buds at the same time. You just need to pause playback on one device before playing it on the other device for that audio to kick in. Now the gaming latency here isn't great. Here's what I got on an iPhone 15 Pro and Nothing Phone 3a Pro. Casual games, you should be fine. If you're into quick reaction games like shooters, not great. All right. Now, category six is core quality. I always run through three tests here. a quiet room, simulation, crowd noise, and a simulation windy city with construction noise. And this is an immense upgrade over Gen One, scoring the QC2 a 9 out of 10. So, three points higher. Bose has gone from the worst call quality to some of the best. And especially with that Windy City test, that's going to be the most noticeable improvement. So, now I'll leave you with some samples here comparing Gen 1 and Gen 2. I'm also going to chuck in one of the highest scored, nothing ear. All right, here is a Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra in a quiet room. All right, here's a Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra 2 in a quiet room. All right, here's a Nothing Ear3 in a quiet room. >> All right, now we're going to see how the microphones block out some simulation crowd noise. All right, here's a Bose Fly Comfort Ultra with some simulation crowd noise. >> All right, here's a Bose Fly Comfort Ultra 2 with some simulation crowd noise. >> All right, here's a Nothing E3 with some simulation crowd noise. >> All right, now I have some city noise, some construction noise. and some simulation wind. >> All right, here's a Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra with some city noise, construction noise, and some simulation wind. >> All right, here's a Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra 2 with some city noise, construction noise, and some simulation wind. >> All right, here's a Nothing E3 with some city noise, construction noise, and some simulation wind. >> All right, now category 7 is app customization and extra features. Scoreing the QC2 a 6.5 out of 10. The app works fine. you just don't get the best customization. First up, you have modes, which basically lets you choose some custom noise cancelling and transparency settings. You can also add spatial audio into the mix and change this with your controls. Source has all your connected devices. With the sound customization, you're only getting a threeb EQ, which is why I brought the score down quite a bit. You do get some freedom to tail the sound, but you need at least a five band EQ nowadays. Immersive audio is basically your spatial audio toggle. The shortcut has your control customization I ran through before. That settings tab at the top right has smaller extras like an ear tip fit test updates. You can turn off touch controls, adjust self voice. So this is how much of your own voice you hear on a phone call. You can still just turn noise cancelling on transparency on which I prefer. You also get some voice prompt settings. And that's basically everything. Again, the same as Gen One. All right. Now, category 8 is noise cancelling. And I test this on a soundbar, simulation, plane noise, crowd noise, random music, and I test all my earbuds at the gym as well. And Bose reclaims that first spot with some of the strongest noise cancelling you can get, scoring them a 10 out of 10. If you watch my AirPods Pro 3 review, you would have seen I chucked the QC2 into that video cuz I needed to compare the best of the best and gone back and forth for basically an hour straight. It's basically neck and neck. It's really close between both buds. It's very impressive noise cancelling. What I find really impressive with the QC2 is the noise cancelling is this strong without any use of foam ear tips like the AirPods Pro 3. So if you did have some foam ear tips, which you could technically chuck on the QC2, you get even stronger noise cancelling. So it's only slightly stronger than Gen 1. Definitely not a massive improvement. That's still incredibly strong noise cancelling as well. But what they have fixed here is that annoying static sound that a lot of people had and I definitely had in my left earbud with noise cancelling on. So, this was noticeable if you listen at low volumes or you had no music playing. There's just constantly an obvious static sound in the left earbud. Now, that has basically almost completely been fixed on Gen 2. I noticed it randomly, but very subtly, like to the point where even if I'm in a really quiet environment, it was pretty tricky to hear. Still, it really shouldn't be there at all, but it's not a huge issue. Now, the rest of it's all the same here. There's barely any EQ shift. You can use one earbud with noise cancelling with the other in the case. And the wind pickup with noise cancelling on is very low. So, if you're outside on a pretty windy day, you're not going to hear the microphones pick up that wind. All right, category 9 is a transparency mode. Scoring the QC2 a 9 out of 10. Again, it's the same as Gen One here. The overall clarity is decent. It's quite a natural transparency mode. Nothing's overly amplified. The occlusion is quite minimal, so I don't get too much of that blocked ear effect, and my own voice sounds quite natural when I speak myself. There isn't too much white noise. You will notice it in a quiet room, but that's the case with any transparency mode. You also get the same active sense mode, which is basically a blend of transparency and noise cancelling. I still think Apple does this the best. So, with the Bose Buds, like if I was around the house and I would turn a tap on, it wouldn't react to that noise where a bud like the AirPods Pro 2 or Pro 3 would. But, you're still able to customize the strength of the noise cancelling as well as the transparency and save separate presets. So, you technically can't turn noise cancelling off, but you can mimic it if you just have it in the middle setting there. All right, now onto the final round. Sound quality. Starting here with my max volume test. Bose knows how to make some loud earbuds. I measured the QC2 just over 119 dB. So, they definitely go loud, but what's also impressive to see here is the low volume EQ. So, naturally, we hear less bass and treble at lower volumes. And Bose kind of compensates for this by boosting the bass and treble. So, you just get a richer sound at the low volumes. I think they boost the bass slightly too much. A bud like the AirPods Pro 3 is a bit more balanced in this regard, but it's still nice that they do it. It would be nice to have the option to turn this off though if you don't like that boosting. But now onto the sound quality itself. I'm scoring the QC2 an 8 out of 10. Again, the sound is basically the same as Gen 1. I think the treble may be boosted slightly more, but definitely not enough of a change to warrant a higher score, but really no complaints with the sound here. I like the Bose tuning. It's rich, but also quite balanced. There is an emphasis on subbase, but it's not over the top. The midbase is quite clean. It can be a bit lacking at times. So, some bass lines and low end from drums and distorted guitars isn't as rich as I would personally like. The mids are definitely a standout though. So, vocals are quite forward but not pushed to the point of being over the top. My only complaint with the tuning here is that the lower mids are a bit lacking. So, I noticed with male vocals or vocals in those lower ranges just lacked a bit of warmth and aren't as rich as other earbuds. The upper mids kind of make up for this though. So instrumentation like keys, snare, drums, percussion, distorted guitars are really well separated. I think this is why the vocals also cut through quite well in those higher ranges and female vocals especially. Now the treble here also has nice balance. You get some precision and detail. It's quite a laid-back treble response. So it's definitely not a fatiguing tuning. The upper treble is a bit lacking though, so crashes, high hats, and like really high ranged vocals and instrumentation does fall back ever so slightly. And this kind of adds to a slightly closedin sound. And since you only get a threeb EQ, it's kind of tricky to improve the upper treble and the lower mids since you only get three bands in there. So if you had a better EQ, I would probably score these buds an 8.5, but I score all the buds on my ranking with only their dedicated apps. Despite all that though, I think it's a pretty well-rounded tuning. And I'll q

Yeah so I've owned these headphones for about 3 months and overall I like them. They're the best Bose headphones I've owned. But there was always something about them that either made me return them or sell them after 4 or 5 months. Usually it was the performance of the app. But I am surprised to see how well this app has been performing. Call quality. It is very clear. Focus is on my voice. They're just a little lower sounding than my other headphones. The multi-connection is very easy. I use them all the time on Microsoft teams at my job. Very comfortable. For my head. I have to get it at the right spot but once I get it at the right spot it stays there. Sound quality, meh. It just depends on what I am listening to. These are probably fine for those who really don't like the abuse to be really loud in their ears, but they want to be able to play their music for a long time. But you're correct, the app doesn't do much for the equalization. The EQ is actually kind of weird on this device. But when I listen to anything other than music, they are perfectly fine. I actually love using Cinema mode when I'm watching certain YouTube videos and movies. ANC and transparency are excellent. I would definitely recommend buying these when they go on sale. $450, that's a little steep. $350 is more of The Sweet spot for me. I tested several headphones from several manufacturers:
Sony XM6, B&W S2, Bose QC Ultra 2nd, Beyerdynamic 300, Apple pro max , SONOS , Audio technica
I was surprised to find that the most important parameter is how they sit on the head, how you can listen for a long time. And this is the most essential parameter. In terms of sound - they all produce wonderful sound, the headphones do not replace listening in the room. The main use is for outdoor activities (and flights). The BOSE is the most comfortable by an order of magnitude. So if headphones like Sony or Apple have one or another feature when walking or flying - it is not interesting and important to me. In my humble opinion, especially for those who wear glasses, the most critical parameter is to take the headphones and put them on your head for about 10 minutes and see how comfortable they really are for you. I put in the effort to find places and take measurements.

Speaking softly

ranked.transcript: Here's my honest review of the Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra second gen with comparisons against the previous first generation and most of its direct competitors including the AirPods Max, Sony XM6, Bowers and Wilkins P7S3 and more. Hey there. If you're new to recording now.com, we purchase every single product we review on a mission to build the largest database of honest headphone and wireless earbud reviews, detailed head-to-head comparisons, and overall rankings to help you make a more informed purchase. I use my background in pro audio to create custom EQ settings for all of these headphones, including the QC Ultra first and second gen to get the most sound quality potential out of them. To access that, visit the full blog post in the video description and in the pin comment below or visit recording now.com. Scroll down to the sound quality section or use the chatbot in the bottom right to type in the model name plus EQ. All right, so the Bose Quiet Comfort Ultra second gen. This is a much anticipated update to the previously excellent all-arounder, the first gen. And in this video, I'm going to answer the question of whether you should upgrade if you own the first gen or which one to get in 2025. So, starting with comfort, the Ultra 2 actually weighed in 8 g heavier than the previous gen 1 at 262 g. That said, the design is pretty identical to the first gen. The only difference that I see cosmetically is that the hinges are this shinier finish versus the more satin or matte finish of the original. When I feel the headband and ear pad cushions, they feel pretty identical. And the interior earup space, which is really nice, by the way, compared to something like the Sony XM6 that is very shallow. And my ears are pressing against the fabric in here. My ears are nice and free inside of these ear cups on both the QC Ultra 1 and 2. So overall for comfort, nothing has been changed. This is a perfect five out of five for comfort. Super lightweight compared to something like the AirPods Max at 387 g. That's over 120 g heavier than the QC Ultra which you surely feel back to back. All right, so moving on to features, build quality, battery life, and noise cancelling performance, which will combine into a single score. Now look at these two headphones. It's easy to think because they have an identical design, all they did was change the finish on the headband and on the hinges. But when you actually spend time with the QC Ultra 2 and you look deeper, there's actually a bunch of nice quality of life improvements. So, let's start by talking about Bluetooth 5.4 versus 5.3. I never had any multi-point connectivity issues with Gen 1. So, it's nice to see that they've upgraded the Bluetooth once again. And if there's any issues, they can always do firmware updates, which I've done a number of those on both the Bose headphones and the Bose QC Ultra earbuds as well. Now, one cool feature that is new for the Ultra 2 is the new cinema mode to match up with something like the Sony XM6's very excellent cinema mode. And in addition to that, there is the immersive audio, which is really Bose's spatial audio, just like Gen 1, where you can have the headtracked or the still or turn it off completely, which I typically do. Another new feature for the QC Ultra 2 is we now have lossless listening via USBC wired connection. This is something similar to the AirPods Max USBC version. Now, moving on to battery life. This has been nicely improved to 30 hours versus the previous 24 hours on Gen 1. So, 6 more hours on a single charge is a nice welcome improvement. Now, for build quality, again, identical design. So, the build quality on the QC Ultra I would say is about average to slightly above average. What I do like about these headphones is they are using metal clearly here in the headband and in the hinges. for being so lightweight, they do feel fairly durable, especially compared to something like the Sony XM6 that just feels like plastic all around. What I don't like about the QC Ultras is they use a ton of plastic here for the ear cup and for the exterior of the headband. So, they actually feel a little cheaper than the price suggests. Something like the AirPods Max does look and feel more premium. Now, moving on to the noise cancelling performance, which I know a lot of you are highly interested. What's the difference between Gen 1 and Gen 2? So upon initial listening, I actually found the noise cancelling to be pretty much the same as the Gen 1. But as I spent more time with the Gen 2, I would say that the biggest caveat to Gen 1 is that there were some side effects to this noise cancelling. Namely, a little bit of hiss or white noise. And when you're moving around or walking around, there was this slight bumping side effect at times due to the noise cancelling. I would say that these things have been improved with the new second gen. I just used these for a workout yesterday and I noticed that they seem to be more consistent with the noise cancelling effectiveness and the lack of side effects. Overall for noise cancelling, I found that the QC Ultra to be one of the best in the class. I do think something like the AirPods Max is really competitive here, perhaps even slightly more effective. So, in the past, the AirPods Max that I've shown in reviews is the original lightning version, but for some reason, this new USBC version, I don't know if it's due to unit versus unit quality control or a different firmware, but the noise cancelling is very strong and effective. Arguably the best I have tested with this AirPods Max. And aside from the Bose QC Ultra and the AirPods Max, I do think that the Sony XM6 and the Sonos Ace after the firmware update are the best noise cancelling headphones in the market right now. I do find the Sony XM6 to be slightly more consistent as far as having even less of those side effects I had mentioned. But overall, when it comes to noise cancelling, Bose is one of the best in the world. I don't think you can go wrong with any of these options right now if noise cancelling is one of your top priorities. So overall for features, battery life, build quality, noise cancelling performance, I give the Bose QC Ultra second gen a perfect five out of five. I had given the Gen 1 a five out of five due to having one of the best noise cancelling, but I may have to slightly alter the score to a 4.5 now that the second gen is improved in every single way, including that battery life and noise cancelling performance. All right, so let's move on to sound quality, which is the other factor that I know a lot of you are interested in. So, just to give some quick context about Gen 1. If you're new to the channel or you haven't seen my review or heard me talk about Gen One, this is actually one of my more favorable headphones to listen to, but there's a very important caveat, which is you must apply EQ in order to get the max sound quality out of these. I still remember my first listening impression of the Gen 1. I noted that they were very bass heavy that completely drowned out the mids and the treble, and I almost couldn't listen to them even after just a few minutes. But after tinkering around with the EQ, I was able to uncover some beautiful mids and treble detail that was hiding under that very bloated and overpowering bass and subbase. Now, moving on to the second gen, I immediately noticed a much more pleasing tuning and sound profile right out of the box. These were so much more enjoyable to listen to versus the stock Gen 1. What jumps out to me about the QC Ultra 2 is just having a more balanced tuning and tonality that really focuses on excellent mids and it's very vocal forward and detailed. That's simply something that you couldn't say about Gen 1. Now, Gen 1 after EQ compared to second gen, even completely stock, I noticed that the QC Ultra 2 is able to have everything across the frequency range from bass, mids, and treble, and it's all able to play nicely with each other. Whereas with Gen One, I pretty much have to subdue the bass in order to make room for the mids and the treble, leading to a very detailed, but a sound that is a little bit thin and lacking definitely in the low end. So, the biggest improvement to me is actually the bass on the second gen. It's much bigger and cleaner while still being able to handle and deliver some beautiful detail in the mids and treble. Now, applying some EQ to the QC Ultra 2 is able to make them even more enjoyable to listen to. My V1 that's on the website right now is geared towards pop and hip-hop, and it's made to emphasize that bass even more. So, I would say that's the biggest difference between the FirstG is this is more of a bass heavy and fun headphone in a good way while still retaining that beautiful mid and treble detail that the original first gen had. Now, overall, I'm hearing slightly more resolution, details, and just a fuller sound compared to Gen 1, which sounds a little bit more compressed. Now, when I listen back to back, the one thing I like about the QC Ultra headphones is they have pretty good sound stage for being wireless closedback headphones. A lot of these headphones tend to sound compressed, but these are some of the more open sounding headphones for the class. Now, just to draw a line in the sand, where does the QC Ultra 2 fit in the grand scheme of things? Personally, I find the QC Ultra 2 to be more enjoyable for music listening than the Sony XM6. The Bowowers and Wilkins PX7S3 is very interesting here because I just reviewed the PX8 S2, which I actually have right here. Now, this is clearly in a level above the QC Ultra 2, but I'd also say above the PX7 as well. But what's interesting is listening to these back to back, I actually spent a little more time to create a new EQ to get the PX7 a little closer to the PX8, which I'm pretty happy with. But the QC Ultra 2 is surprisingly competent here. That said, after dialing in EQ for the PX7 to get the most out of it, I will say that the QC Ultra 2 is slightly below the PX7S3, which just excels more for those audio file technicalities. And then one other headphone I've mentioned a lot on this channel is the Cambridge Audio P100, which I believe is also in a slightly level above the PX7 and above the QC Ultra 2 as well. This really competes more with the high-end of the class like the Focal Batiste and even a little bit of the PXA S2. I find the QC Ultra 2 and something like the AirPods Max to be about equally enjoyable, but I think because of that EQ that is very effective for the Bose and it takes EQ fairly well, you're able to have more versatility for the sound overall. And I find the bass to just be very pleasing to listen to. Actually, even more pleasing than the bass on the PX7S3 and the P100. So that's really the strength of these headphones is they're very enjoyable to listen to, especially if it's more consumer friendly and casual listening. So overall, I give the Bose QC Ultra 2 a 9.1 out of 10. This is a nice improvement over the first gen. All right, so moving on to value and answering the question, should you upgrade from Gen 1 to Gen 2? So these currently retail at $449 USD. That is about the same price as the Gen 1 was before, but really interestingly, the Gen 1 actually used to cost closer to $429 and then on sale for as low as $349. But essentially, the price didn't really go up that much compared to Gen 1. And I have to say, if we think about the overall package, having that perfect comfort, having one of the best noise cancelling in the class, having sound quality that is very good and enjoyable, it is really hard to have all of those categories, and to have very little to no weaknesses. So, if you don't own the Gen 1, I will say absolutely if you can afford it, go for the Gen 2 because it's simply better than the Gen 1 in every single way. finalVerdict.summary: Bose QC Ultra Gen2는 노이즈 캔슬링 기능과 편안한 착용감에서 높은 평가를 받고 있으며, 특히 출퇴근이나 항공 여행 시 유용하다. 그러나 기본 음향 튜닝이 저음에 과도하게 의존하고, 앱을 통한 조정 기능이 제한적이라는 점이 단점으로 지적된다. 또한, 일부 사용자는 이어폰이 빠지기 쉬운 문제와 터치 제어 민감도를 지적하고 있으며, 플라스틱 외관이 저렴하게 느껴진다는 의견도 있다. 전반적으로 ANC 기능을 중시하는 사용자에게 추천되며, 순수 음질만을 중시하는 사용자나 기존 Gen1 소유자는 업그레이드를 신중히 고려해야 한다.
top_strengths: USB-C 무선 연결을 통한 무손실 오디오 지원
top_weaknesses: 기본 음향 튜닝이 저음에 과도하게 의존
who_should_buy: 무선 충전 및 멀티포인트 연결 기능을 원하는 사용자
finalVerdict.upgrade_advice: 기존 Bose QC Ultra Gen1 소유자는 Gen2의 노이즈 캔슬링 성능 개선과 무선 충전, 이어팁 디자인 개선 등을 고려해야 하지만, 업그레이드 가치가 제한적이라는 점을 염두에 두어야 한다. 특히, 음질이나 기능적인 변화가 크지 않다면 업그레이드를 신중히 고려하는 것이 좋다.
finalVerdict.buying_advice: 신규 구매자는 노이즈 캔슬링 기능과 편안한 착용감을 중시하는 경우 Bose QC Ultra Gen2를 고려할 수 있다. 그러나 음질 조정 기능의 제한과 일부 사용 환경에서의 단점을 고려하여, 순수 음질이나 고급 외관을 중시하는 사용자는 다른 제품을 고려하는 것이 좋다.
